lines.text: SEGMENT 1 — Embryos without conception
Alright, settle in.

Today, for the next two hours, we're going to talk about one of the most genuinely disorienting developments in modern biology — a line of work that, depending on who you ask, is either the most important advance in developmental biology since the discovery of induced pluripotent stem cells, or a slow-motion ethics crisis that the field is only beginning to grapple with.
Probably both.

The topic is synthetic embryos — structures grown in dishes, from stem cells alone, without an egg, without a sperm, without fertilization, that nonetheless look like embryos and, to a remarkable extent, behave like embryos.
They're called by different names depending on who's doing the work and what the construct contains: blastoids, embryoids, ETX embryos, stembryos, synthetic embryo models.
Underneath all those names is one central, astonishing observation, and it's the observation I want you to walk out of here remembering.
If you walk out of here today and remember nothing else, I want you to remember this single sentence.

You take embryonic stem cells, or induced pluripotent stem cells, or trophoblast stem cells — you plate them together in the right gel with the right signaling cocktail — and they self-organize into structures that look like, and partially behave like, real early embryos.
You don't sculpt them.
You don't print them.
You don't direct individual cells with tweezers.
You provide the substrate and the cues, and the cells self-organize the embryo themselves.
The architectural program for the earliest stages of mammalian development is in the cells.
We just turn them loose and let them rebuild it.
Let me unpack that, because every word matters, and because the implications run pretty deep.
Embryonic stem cells.
These are the cells that, in a real embryo, form the inner cell mass of the blastocyst — the little ball of cells inside the early hollow embryo that goes on to make the body itself.
They're pluripotent: each one can become any tissue in your body, given the right cues.
In a dish, we can grow them essentially forever, and we can push them toward any developmental endpoint we choose.
Induced pluripotent stem cells.
These are adult cells — skin, blood, whatever — that have been reprogrammed back to a pluripotent state by forcing them to express a few key transcription factors.
Yamanaka's work in 2006.
We talked about these in detail in another lecture.
The crucial point for today: iPSCs behave, for most purposes, like embryonic stem cells.
They can also build embryo-like structures, and that fact is important ethically because iPSC-derived synthetic embryos don't trace back to a destroyed embryo.
Trophoblast stem cells.
This one is newer, and it's the missing piece that made the synthetic-embryo field really take off.
Trophoblast is the outer layer of the blastocyst — the cells that go on to form the placenta and the extraembryonic membranes, not the body itself.
For a long time, we could grow embryonic stem cells beautifully in dishes, but we couldn't grow stable trophoblast stem cells, especially from humans.
That meant we could model the part of the embryo that becomes the body, but we couldn't model the part that becomes the placenta.
We were missing half the picture.
The breakthroughs in trophoblast stem cell derivation — first in mouse, then more recently in human — gave us the second ingredient we needed.
The right signaling cocktail.
This is the same idea you've encountered in the organoid lectures.
The cells in an early embryo are talking to each other constantly through a defined set of signaling pathways — Wnt, BMP, Nodal, FGF, Hippo, and a handful of others.
The synthetic-embryo cocktail is, essentially, a reconstitution of those embryonic conversations, mixed up from purified factors and added to the dish.
We're not inventing new signals; we're replaying the signals the embryo uses, in vitro, so the cells think they're inside an embryo.
They self-organize.
This is the word that does the most work.
The cells aren't told where to go.
There's no scaffold pre-shaped like a blastocyst that the cells fill in.
There's no microscale architect arranging the trophoblasts on the outside and the pluripotent cells on the inside.
The cells, given the right starting conditions, work it out themselves.
Trophoblast cells, in the right environment, segregate to the outside of the aggregate.
Pluripotent cells cluster on the inside.
A cavity forms.
A polarized inner cell mass emerges.
The whole thing organizes itself into something that, under the microscope, looks like a blastocyst — sometimes indistinguishably so.
Now, the obvious question.
Why?
Why have we spent the last decade figuring out how to grow these things?
Three answers.
First: early-embryo research is ethically and technically limited.
Real human embryos, in the first two weeks of development, are exquisitely difficult to work with.
They exist for a few days after fertilization and then implant into the uterine wall, where they become inaccessible.
Researchers have, in some jurisdictions, been allowed to study embryos donated from IVF clinics — but only up to a strict time limit, with strict oversight, and only in tiny numbers.

The biology of the first two weeks of human development — the period when most miscarriages happen, when most early developmental abnormalities arise, when the basic body plan is being laid down — is, despite a century of work, still mysterious in important ways.
Synthetic embryos, derived from stem cells, give us a system to study this period without using real embryos.
Second: synthetic embryos offer experimental access that real embryos simply don't allow.
You can make hundreds of them.
You can knock out genes in them.
You can dose them with drugs.
You can image them around the clock.
You can vary the conditions.

You can run experiments at a scale and rigor that's simply impossible with the precious, rare, ethically constrained real human embryos that occasionally make their way into research labs from IVF donation.
Third: synthetic embryos blur the definition of "embryo," and that, for better or worse, is reshaping bioethics in real time.

If a structure has trophoblast on the outside, pluripotent cells on the inside, can implant into the uterine wall of a mouse, can develop heart and brain — at what point does it count as an embryo for ethical purposes?
Different countries, different ethics committees, different scientific societies are giving different answers, and we're going to walk through the landscape carefully.
So here's our trajectory for today.
We'll start with the central idea — what a synthetic embryo is and how the field thinks about it.

We'll work through the missing piece that held the field back for years: the absence of stable trophoblast stem cells, and the breakthroughs by Kathy Niakan in 2014 and Hiroaki Okae in 2018 that finally gave us those cells.
We'll walk through Nicolas Rivron's 2018 mouse blastoid paper — the first proof that the system could work.
Then the Magdalena Zernicka-Goetz ETX embryoids, the Polo and Hanna lab human blastoids, the Hanna lab synthetic mouse embryos that develop to day 8.5 in vitro with hearts and brains.
Then we pivot into bioethics — the 14-day rule, the 2021 ISSCR update, the philosophical question of what "embryo" even means.
And we'll end on the frontier: extending the time window, organogenesis-in-a-dish, the eventual question of in-vitro gestation.

By the end, you should be able to explain to a friend at dinner what a synthetic embryo is, why it's scientifically interesting, why it's ethically fraught, and why the existence of embryo-like structures grown from skin cells is going to be one of the defining bioethical questions of the next decade.
Let's begin.
